data.summary: AgentBook is a text-based PDF designed for testing various functionalities such as document parsing, chunking, embedding, vector search, answer generation, summarization, and study-guide endpoints[2]. The system includes components like the Parser Router that selects parsers based on input modality (e.g., Docling, OCR, spreadsheet, audio), Chunk Builder that creates retrieval-ready blocks from these inputs, and a Vector Indexer that embeds chunks into Qdrant for efficient search[8]. The main evaluation rule is to fail loudly when input quality is too poor to process, ensuring that any hallucinated answers are flagged as unreliable[1]. AgentBook also includes features like the Study Guide Agent, which converts source content into learning objectives and quiz items, helping users understand and review the material effectively[8].

citations.snippet_original: The summarize endpoint should produce a concise overview of the material, including system purpose, input modalities, parser behavior, and debug signals. The study-guide endpoint should be more pedagogical: it should list learning objectives, define key terms, propose review questions, and highlight practical implementation risks such as low-quality OCR images or incorrectly chunked tables.

evidence_blocks.snippet_original: The summarize endpoint should produce a concise overview of the material, including system purpose, input modalities, parser behavior, and debug signals. The study-guide endpoint should be more pedagogical: it should list learning objectives, define key terms, propose review questions, and highlight practical implementation risks such as low-quality OCR images or incorrectly chunked tables.

The most important evaluation rule is that a parser should fail loudly when the input is too poor to process. Silent failure is worse than refusal because it allows hallucinated answers to appear trustworthy. For this reason, the test pack includes both pass and fail examples.
Table 2. Evaluation plan for the AgentBook sample pack.

Converts source content into learning objectives and quiz items

This document describes a compact retrieval-augmented learning system named AgentBook. It is intentionally built as a text-based PDF with headings, paragraphs, tables, and figures. The file is suitable for testing document parsing, chunking, embedding, vector search, answer generation, summarization, and study-guide endpoints.

citations.snippet_original: A synthetic English document for testing RAG, summarization, study-guide generation, table retrieval, and cross-lingual Vietnamese-to-English queries.

evidence_blocks.snippet_original: A synthetic English document for testing RAG, summarization, study-guide generation, table retrieval, and cross-lingual Vietnamese-to-English queries.
AgentBook Retrieval-Augmented Learning System: Sample Technical Notes

For cross-lingual testing, a user may ask a Vietnamese question over this English document. A robust system should retrieve the relevant English evidence, then answer naturally in Vietnamese while preserving factual grounding. The test is not about translation alone; it is about whether retrieval remains stable when the query language differs from the source language.
Figure 1. Synthetic coverage score used for parser smoke testing.

citations.snippet_original: The retrieval pipeline begins with material normalization. Text blocks, table blocks, figure captions, OCR blocks, and audio transcript segments are converted into a common schema. This schema preserves the source page, sheet, timestamp, block type, and confidence score when applicable. The normalized schema is essential because downstream agents should not care whether a fact came from a PDF paragraph or a spreadsheet row.

evidence_blocks.snippet_original: The retrieval pipeline begins with material normalization. Text blocks, table blocks, figure captions, OCR blocks, and audio transcript segments are converted into a common schema. This schema preserves the source page, sheet, timestamp, block type, and confidence score when applicable. The normalized schema is essential because downstream agents should not care whether a fact came from a PDF paragraph or a spreadsheet row.

citations.snippet_original: The central idea is simple: users upload learning materials, the parser converts heterogeneous content into normalized blocks, and a retrieval layer selects evidence for downstream agents. Even though this document is synthetic, its structure imitates a technical paper so that automated test cases can exercise realistic layouts.
